html:
Part II

In London

1912

January 21

Am at last beginning to get more content with the work at the Museum, so that I muse on Bernard ShawвАЩs saying, вАЬGet what you like or youвАЩll grow to like what you get.вАЭ I have a terrible suspicion that the security of tenure here is like the lionвАЩs den in the fableвБ†вАФNulla vestiga retrorsum. Of course I am wonderfully proud of being at the Museum, although I am disappointed and write as if I were quite blas√©.

January 25

I should be disappointed if at the end of my career (if I live to see it through) I do not win the F.R.S. I should very much like it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ My nature is very mixedвБ†вАФambitious above all things and yet soon giddy with the audacity of my aspirations. The B.M. and my colleagues make me feel most inferior in fact, but in theoryвБ†вАФin the secrecy of my own bedchamberвБ†вАФI feel that there are few men there my equal.

April 26

Down with influenza. A boardinghouse with the flu!

May 8

Went home to recuperate, a beef jelly in one pocket and sal volatile in the other. On arrival, my blanched appearance frightened Mother and the others, so went to bed at once. вАЬFateвАЩs a fiddler, lifeвАЩs a dance.вАЭ

May 12

Weak enough to sit down before dressing-table while I shave and brush my hair. Dyspepsia appalling. The Doctor in Kensington seemed to think me an awful wreck and asked if I were concealingвБ†вАФ

Reading Baudelaire and Verlaine.

May 24

Bathing

Sat on a seat overlooking the sand hills with stick between my legs like an old man, and watched a buxom wench aet. twenty-five run down the path pursued by вАЬRoughвАЭ and two little girls in blue. Later they emerged from a striped bathing tent in the glory of blue bathing dresses. It made me feel quite an old man to see the girl galloping out over the hard level sands to the breakers, a child clinging to each hand. Legs and arms twinkled in the sun which shone with brilliance. If life were as level as those sands and as beautiful as that trio of girls!

May 26

Two Young Men Talking

With HвБ†вЄЇ in his garden. He is a great enthusiast.

вАЬI disapprove entirely of your taste in gardening,вАЭ I said. вАЬYou object to the вАШragged wildernessвАЩ style, I like it. You like lawns laid out for croquet and your privet hedges pruned into вАШGod Save the KingвАЩ or вАШDieu et mon droit.вАЩ My dear boy, if you saw Mr.¬†вЄївАЩs wilderness at вЄї youвАЩd be so shocked youвАЩd cut and run, and I imagine thereвАЩd be an affecting reunion between you and your beloved geraniums. For my part, I donвАЩt like geraniums: theyвАЩre suburban, and all of a piece with antimacassars and stuffed birds under glass bells. The colour of your specimens, moreover,вАЭ I rapped out, вАЬis vulgarвБ†вАФlike the muddied petticoats of old market women.вАЭ

HвБ†вЄЇ, quite unmoved, replied slowly, вАЬWell, here are some like the beautiful white cambric of a lady of fashion. YouвАЩve got no taste in flowersвБ†вАФyouвАЩre just six feet of grief and patience.вАЭ We roared with laughing.

вАЬDo stop watering those damned plants,вАЭ I exclaimed at last. But he went on. I exclaimed again and out of sheer ridiculousness, in reply he proceeded to water the cabbages, the gravel path, the oak treeвБ†вАФand me! While I writhed with laughing.

May 27

By the Sea

Sat upon a comfortable jetty of rock and watched the waves without a glimmer of an idea in my mind about anythingвБ†вАФthough to outward view I might have been a philosopher in cerebral parturition with thoughts as big as babies. Instead, little rustling dead leaves of thoughts stirred and fluttered in the brainвБ†вАФthe pimple e.g. I recollected on my AuntвАЩs nose, or the boyishness of Dr.¬†вЄївАЩs handwriting, or SwinburneвАЩs lines: вАЬIf the golden-crested wren / Were a nightingaleвБ†вАФwhy, then / Something seen and heard of men / Might be half as sweet as when / Laughs a child of seven.вАЭ

I continued in this pleasurable coma all the afternoon and went home refreshed.

May 29

Have returned to London and the B.M. My first day at the M. Sat at my table in a state of awful apathy.

At least temporarily, I am quite disenchanted of Zoology. I workвБ†вАФGod save the markвБ†вАФin the Insect Room!

On the way home, purchased:вБ†вАФ

Peroxide of hydrogen (pyorrhoea threatened). One bottle of physic (for my appalling dyspepsia).

One flask of brandy for emergencies (as my heart is intermittent again).

Prussic acid next.

Must have been near pneumonia at RвБ†вЄЇ. Auntie was nervous, and came in during the night to see how I was.

June 20

It caused me anguish to see my article returned from the Fortnightly and lying in a big envelope on the table when I returned home this evening. I canвАЩt do any work because of it, and in desperation rushed off to the stately pleasure domes of the White City, and systematically went through all the thrillsвБ†вАФfrom the Mountain Railway to the Wiggle Woggle and the вАЩWitching Waves.

June 21

Today I am easier. The cut worm forgives the plough. But how restless this disappointment has made me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I have no plans for recuperation and cannot settle down to work.

July 6

On my doctorвАЩs advice, went to see Dr.¬†PвБ†вЄЇ, a lung specialist. MвБ†вЄЇвБ†found a dull spot on one of my lungs, and, not feeling very sure, and without telling me the nature of his suspicion, he arranged for Dr.¬†PвБ†вЄЇ to see me, allowing me to suppose he was a stomach authority as my dyspepsia is bad.

Well: it is not consumption, but my lungs and physique are such that consumption might easily supervene. As soon as Dr.¬†PвБ†вЄЇ had gone, MвБ†вЄЇ appended the following lugubrious yarn:вБ†вАФ

Whenever I catch cold, I must go and be treated at once, all my leisure must be spent out of doors, I must take cream and milk in prodigious quantities and get fat at all costs. There is even a question of my giving up work.

July 10

A young but fat woman sitting in the sun and oozing moisture is as nasty as anything in Baudelaire.

July 14

A вАЬBrilliant CareerвАЭ

My old head master once prophesied for me вАЬa brilliant career.вАЭ That was when I was in the Third Form. Now I have more than a suspicion that I am one of those who, as he once pointed out, grow sometimes out of a brilliant boyhood into very commonplace men. This continuous ill health is having a very obvious effect on my work and activities. With what courage I possess I have to face the fact that today I am unable to think or express myself as well as when I was a boy in my teensвБ†вАФwitness this Journal!

I intend to go on however. I have decided that my death shall be disputed all the way.

Oh! it is so humiliating to die! I writhe to think of being overcome by so unfair an enemy before I have demonstrated myself to maiden aunts who mistrust me, to colleagues who scorn me, and even to brothers and sisters who believe in me.

As an Egotist I hate death because I should cease to be I.

Most folk, when sick unto death, gain a little consolation over the notoriety gained by the fact of their decease. Criminals enjoy the pomp and circumstance of their execution. Voltaire said of Rousseau that he wouldnвАЩt mind being hanged if theyвАЩd stick his name on the gibbet. But my own death would be so mean and insignificant. Guy de Maupassant died in a grand mannerвБ†вАФa man of intellect and splendid physique who became insane. TusitalaвАЩs death in the South Seas reads like a romance. Heine, after a life of sorrow, died with a sparkling witticism on his lips; Vespasian with a jest.

But I cannot for the life of me rake up any excitement over my own immediate deceaseвБ†вАФan unobtrusive passing away of a rancorous, disappointed, morbid, and self-assertive entomologist in a West Kensington Boarding HouseвБ†вАФwhat a mean little tragedy! It is hard not to be somebody even in death.

A singsong tonight in the drawing-room; all the boardinghouse present in full muster. There was a German, Schulz, who sat and leered at his inamorataвБ†вАФa sensual-looking, pasty-faced girlвБ†вАФwhile she gave us daggers-and-moonlight recitations with the most unwarranted self-assurance (she boasts of a walking-on part at one of the theatres); there was Miss MвБ†вЄЇ listening to her fianc√©, Capt.¬†OвБ†вЄЇ (home from India), singing Indian Love Songs at her; there was Miss TвБ†вЄЇ, a sour old maid, who knitted and snorted, not fully conscious of this young blood coursing around her; Mrs.¬†Barclay Woods pursued her usual avocation of imposing on us all the great weight of her immense social superiority, clucking, in between, to her one chickвБ†вАФa fluffy girl of eighteen or nineteen, who was sitting now in the draught, now too close to a вАЬcommonвАЭ musician of the Covent Garden Opera; finally our hostess, a divorcee, who hated all males, even Tomcats. We were a pathetic little companyвБ†вАФso motley, ill-assortedвБ†вАФwho had come together not from love or regard but because man is a gregarious animal. In fact, we sat secretly criticising and contemning one anotherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ yet outside there were so many millions of people unknown, and overhead the multitude of the stars was equally comfortless.

Later:вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Zoology on occasion still fires my ambition! Surely I cannot be dying yet.

Whatever misfortune befalls me I do hope I shall be able to meet it unflinchingly. I do not fear ill-health in itself, but I do fear its possible effect on my mind and character.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Already I am slowly altering, as the Lord liveth. Already for example my sympathy with myself is maudlin.

Whenever the blow shall fall, some sort of a reaction must be given. Heine flamed into song. Beethoven wrote the 5th Symphony. So what shall I do when my time comes? I donвАЩt think I have any lyrics or symphonies to write, so I shall just have to grin and bear itвБ†вАФlike a dumb animal.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ As long as I have spirit and buoyancy I donвАЩt care what happensвБ†вАФfor I know that for so long I cannot be accounted a failure. The only real failure is one in which the victim is left spiritless, dazed, dejected with blackness all around, and within, a knife slowly and unrelentingly cutting the strings of his heart.

My head whirls with conflicting emotions, struggling, desperate ideas, and a flood of impressions of all sorts of things that are never sufficiently sifted and arranged to be caught down on paper. I am brought into this world, hustled along it and then hustled out of it, with no time for anything. I want to be on a great hill and square up affairs.

August 28

вА¶ After tea, we all three walked in Kensington Gardens and sat on a seat by the Round Pond. My umbrella fell to the ground, and I left it there with its nose poking up in a cynical manner, as She remarked.

вАЬItвАЩs not cynical,вАЭ I said, вАЬonly a little knowing. WonвАЩt you let yours fall down to keep it company? Yours is a lady umbrella and a good-looking oneвБ†вАФthey might flirt together.вАЭ

вАЬMine doesnвАЩt want to flirt,вАЭ she answered stiffly.

September 13

At CвБ†вЄЇ, a tiny little village by the sea in NвБ†вЄЇ.

Looking up from a rock pool, where I had been watching Gobies, I saw three children racing across the sands to bathe, I saw a man dive from a boat, and I saw a horseman gallop his mare down to the beach and plunge about in the line of breakers. The waters thundered, the mare whinnied, the children shouted to one another, and I turned my head down again to the rock pool with a great thumping heart of happiness: it was so lovely to be conscious of the fact that out there this beautiful picture was awaiting me whenever and as often as I chose to lift my head. I purposely kept my head down, for the picture was so beautiful I did not want to hurt it by breathing on it, and I kept my head down out of a playful self-cheating delight; I decided not to indulge myself.

September 16

Out in the Bay dredging for Echinoderms with вАЬCarrots.вАЭ Brilliantly fine. The haul was a failure, but, being out in a boat on a waveless sea under a cloudless sky, I was scarcely depressed at this! We cruised along from one little bay to another, past smugglersвАЩ caves and white pebble beaches, the dredge all the while growling along the sea bottom, and вАЬCarrotsвАЭ and I lying listless in the bows. I was immensely happy. My mercury was positively ringing the bell.

Who, then, is вАЬCarrotsвАЭ? He is a fine brawny boatman who jumps over the rocks like a Chamois, swims like a Fish, pulls like an Ox, snorts like a GrampusвБ†вАФa sort of compound zoological perfection, built eclectically.

September 18

Early Boughies

Up the village, Mrs.¬†Beavan keeps a tiny little shop and runs a very large garden. She showed us all about the garden, and introduced us to her husband, whom we discovered in an apple treeвБ†вАФan old man, aged seventy-six, very hard of hearing, and with an impediment in his speech. He at once began to move his mouth, and I caught odd jingles of sound that sounded like nothing at allвБ†вАФat first, but which gradually resolved themselves on close attention to such familiar landmarks as вАЬEarly Boughies,вАЭ вАЬStubbits,вАЭ вАЬRibstone PippinsвАЭ into a discourse on Apples.

The following curious conversation took place between me and the deaf gaffer, aged seventy-six, standing in the apple treeвБ†вАФ

вАЬThese be all appulls from KentвБ†вАФI got вАЩem all from Kent.вАЭ

вАЬHow long have you lived in CвБ†вЄЇ?вАЭ

вАЬBunyard¬†& SonвБ†вАФthatвАЩs the firmвБ†вАФthey live just outside the town of Maidstone.вАЭ

вАЬDo you keep Bees here?вАЭ

вАЬOne of these yer appulls is called Bunyard after the firmвБ†вАФa fine fruit too.вАЭ

вАЬYour good wife must be of great assistance to you in your work.вАЭ

вАЬLittle stalks maybe, but a large juishy appull for all that.вАЭ

Just then I heard Mrs.¬†BвБ†вЄЇ saying to EвБ†вЄЇвБ†вАФ

вАЬAw yes, heвАЩs very active for seventy-six. A little deaf, but he manages the garden all вАЩeesulf, I bolsters вАЩun up wiвАЩ meat and drinkвБ†вАФlittle and often as they zay for children.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Now thereвАЩs a bootifull tree, me dear, that вАЩas almost beared itself to death, as you may say.вАЭ

She picked an apple off it shouting to poor Tom still aloftвБ†вАФ

вАЬTom whatвАЩs the name of this one?вАЭ

вАЬYou should come a bit earlier, zir,вАЭ replied T. вАЬвАКвАЩTis late a bit now doanвАЩt вАЩee zee?вАЭ

вАЬNoвБ†вАФwhatвАЩs its name I want,вАЭ shouted his spouse.

вАЬYes, yes, give the lady one to take homeвБ†вАФthereвАЩs plenty for all,вАЭ he said.

вАЬWhat is the name? The name of the yer appull,вАЭ screamed Mrs.¬†B., and old Tom moving his bones slowly down from the tree answered quite unmovedвБ†вАФ

вАЬAw the name? Why, вАЩtis a common kind of appullвБ†вАФthereвАЩs a nice tree of вАЩem up there.вАЭ

вАЬOh! never mind, вАЩtis a Gladstone,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†B., turning to us.

вАЬA very fine Appull,вАЭ droned the old boy.

September 28

Back in town again. Wandered about in a somnambulistic way all the afternoon till I found myself taking tea in Kew Gardens. I enjoyed the wind in my face and hair. Otherwise there is nothing to be saidвБ†вАФa colourless day.

October 10

Came across the following arresting sentence: вАЬPale, anaemic, cadaverous, bad teeth and disordered digestion and a morbid egotism.вАЭ Yes, but my teeth are not bad.

October 20

On the N. Downs

Under the oak where I sat the ground was covered with dead leaves. I kicked them, and I beat them with my stick, because I was angry that they were dead. In the coppice, leaves were quietly and majestically floating earthwards in the pomp of death. It was very thrilling to observe them.

It was a curious sensation to realise that since the last time I sat under the old oak I had been right up to the N. of England, then right down to the S.W., and back once more to London town. I bragged about my kinetic activity to the stationary oak and I scoffed at the old hill for having to remain always in the same place.

It gave me a pleasing sense of infinite superiority to come back and see everything the same as before, to sit on the same old seat under the same old oak. Even that same old hurdle was lying in the same position among the bracken. How sorry I was for it! Poor wretchвБ†вАФunable to moveвБ†вАФto go to Whitby, to go to CвБ†вЄЇ, to be totally ignorant of the great country of London.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Day dreamed. My own life as it unrolls day by day is a source of constant amazement, delight, and pain. I can think of no more interesting volume than a detailed, intimate, psychological history of my own life. I want a perfect comprehension at least of myself.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

We are all such egotists that a sorrow or hardshipвБ†вАФprovided it is great enoughвБ†вАФflatters our self-importance. We feel that a calamity by overtaking us has distinguished us above our fellows. A man likes not to be ignored even by a railway accident. A man with a grievance is always happy.

October 23

Over to see EвБ†вЄЇ. Came away disillusioned.

October 25

Met her in SmithвАЩs book shop looking quite bewitching. Hang it all, I thought I had finished. Went home with her, watched her make a pudding in the kitchen, then we sat by the firelight in the drawing-room and had supper. Scrumptious (not the supper).

October 27

Quarrelled with DвБ†вЄЇ! The atmosphere is changed at the flatвБ†вАФmy character is ruined. DвБ†вЄЇ has told them IвАЩm a loose fellow. IвАЩve always contrived to give him that impressionвБ†вАФI liked to be cutting my throatвБ†вАФand now itвАЩs cut!

November 1

DвБ†вЄЇ came and carried me off to the flat, where they asked why I hadnвАЩt been overвБ†вАФwhich, of course, pleased me immensely.

November 6

Doctor MвБ†вЄЇ is very gloomy about my health and talks of S. Africa, Labrador, and so on. IвАЩm not responding to his treatment as I should.

November 11

Met her this evening in Kensington Road. вАЬI timed this well,вАЭ said she, вАЬI thought I should meet you.вАЭ Good Heavens, I am getting embroiled. Returned to the flat with her and after supper called her вАЬThe Lady of Shalott.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt think you know what youвАЩre talking aboutвАЭвБ†вАФthis stiffly.

вАЬPerhaps not,вАЭ I answered. вАЬI leave it to you.вАЭ

вАЬOh! but it rests with you,вАЭ she said.

Am I in love? God knowsвБ†вАФbut I donвАЩt suppose God cares.

November 15

On MвБ†вЄЇвАЩs advice went to see a stomach specialistвБ†вАФDr.¬†Hawkins. As I got there a little too early walked up the streetвБ†вАФPortland PlaceвБ†вАФon the opposite side (from shyness) past an interminable and nauseating series of night bells and brass plates, then down again on the right side till I got to No.¬†66 which made me flutterвБ†вАФfor ten doors ahead I mused is the house I must call at. It made me shiver a little.

The specialist took copious notes of my evidence and after examining me retired to consult with MвБ†вЄЇ. What a parade of ceremony! On coming back, the jury returned a verdict of вАЬNot proven.вАЭ I was told I ought to go out and live on the prairiesвБ†вАФand in two years I should be a giant! But where are the prairies? What bus? If I get worse, I must take several monthsвАЩ leave. I think it will come to this.

November 16

Arthur came down for the weekend. He likes the Lady of Shalott. She is вАЬnot handsome, but arresting, strikingвАЭ and вАЬcapable of tragedy.вАЭ That I believe she has achieved already.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If she were a bit more gloomy and a bit more beautiful, sheвАЩd be irresistible.

November 22

He: вАЬHave a cigarette? I enjoy lighting your cigarettes.вАЭ

She: вАЬI donвАЩt know how to smoke properly.вАЭ

He: вАЬYou smoke only as you could.вАЭ

She: вАЬHowвАЩs that?вАЭ

H.: вАЬGracefully, of course.вАЭ

S.: вАЬDo you think I like pretty things being said to me?вАЭ

H.: вАЬWhy not, if they are true. Flattery is when you tell an ugly woman she is beautiful. Have you so poor an opinion of yourself to think all I say of you is flattery?вАЭ

S.: вАЬYes. I am only four bare wallsвБ†вАФwith nothing inside.вАЭ

H.: вАЬWhat a deliciously empty feeling that must be.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But I donвАЩt think youвАЩre so simple as all that. You bewilder me sometimes.вАЭ

S.: вАЬWhy?вАЭ

H.: вАЬI feel like Sindbad the Sailor.вАЭ

S.: вАЬWhy?вАЭ

H.: вАЬBecause IвАЩm not George Meredith.вАЭ The title of вАЬhusbandвАЭ frightens me.

December 9

ItвАЩs a fearful strain to go on endeavouring to live up to time with a carefully laid-out timetable of future achievements. I am hurrying on with my study of Italian in order to read the Life of Spallanzani in order to include him in my bookвБ†вАФto be finished by the end of next year; I am also subsidising JenkinsonвАЩs embryological lectures at University College with the more detailed account of practical and experimental work in his textbook; I have also started a lengthy research upon the TrichopteraвБ†вАФall with a horrible sense of time fleeing swiftly and opportunities for work too few ever to be squandered, and, in the background, behind all this feverish activity, the black shadow that I might die suddenly with nothing doneвБ†вАФnext year, next month, next week, tomorrow, now!

Then sometimes, as tonight, I have misgivings. Shall I do these things so well now as I might once have done them? Has not my ill-health seriously affected my mental powers? Surely the boy of 1908вБ†вАУвБ†10 was almost a genius orвБ†вАФseen at this distanceвБ†вАФa very remarkable youth in the fanatical zeal with which he sought to pursue, and succeeded in gaining, his own end of a zoological education for himself.

It is a terrible suspicion to cross the mind of an ambitious youth that perhaps, after all, he is a very commonplace mortalвБ†вАФthat his life, whether comedy or tragedy, or both, or neither, is anyway insignificant, of no account.

It is still more devastating for him to have to consider whether the laurel wreath was not once within his grasp, and whether he must not ascribe his own incalculable loss to his stomach simply.

December 15

A very bad heart attack. As I write it intermits every three or four beats. Who knows if I shall live through tonight?

December 16

Here I am once more. A passable night. After breakfast the intermittency recommencedвБ†вАФit is better now, with a dropped beat only about once per half-hour, so that I am almost happy after yesterday, which was Hell. The world is too good to give up without remonstrance at the beck of a weak heart.

Before I went to sleep last night, my watch stoppedвБ†вАФI at once observed the cessation of its tick and wondered if it were an omen. I was genuinely surprised to find myself still ticking when I awoke this morning. A moment ago a hearse passed down the street.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yes, but IвАЩm damned if I havenвАЩt a right to be morbid after yesterday. To be ill like this in a boardinghouse! IвАЩd marry tomorrow if I had the chance.

December 22

SollasвАЩs Ancient Hunters

Read SollasвАЩs book Ancient HuntersвБ†вАФvery thrillingвБ†вАФmind full of the Aurignacians, Mousterians, Magdalenians! I have been peering down such tremendous vistas of time and change that my own troubles have been eclipsed into ridiculous insignificance. It has been really a Pillar of Strength to meвБ†вАФa splendid tonic. Paleontology has its comfortable words too. I have revelled in my littleness and irresponsibility. It has relieved me of the harassing desire to live, I feel content to live dangerously, indifferent to my fate; I have discovered I am a fly, that we are all flies, that nothing matters. ItвАЩs a great load off my life, for I donвАЩt mind being such a microorganismвБ†вАФto me the honour is sufficient of belonging to the universeвБ†вАФsuch a great universe, so grand a scheme of things. Not even Death can rob me of that honour. For nothing can alter the fact that I have lived; I have been I, if for ever so short a time. And when I am dead, the matter which composes my body is indestructibleвБ†вАФand eternal, so that come what may to my вАЬSoul,вАЭ my dust will always be going on, each separate atom of me playing its separate partвБ†вАФI shall still have some sort of a finger in the Pie. When I am dead, you can boil me, burn me, drown me, scatter meвБ†вАФbut you cannot destroy me: my little atoms would merely deride such heavy vengeance. Death can do no more than kill you.

December 27

вАЬIt is a pleasure to note the success attending the career of Mr.¬†W. N. P. Barbellion now engaged in scientific work on the staff of the Natural History MuseumвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ etc., etc.

This is a cutting from the local paperвБ†вАФone of many that from time to time I once delightedly pasted in the pages of the Journal. Not so now.

вА¶ At twenty-three, I am a different being. Surrounded by all the stimulating environment of scientific research, I am cold and disdainful. I keep up the old appearances but underneath it is quite different. I am a hypocrite. I have to wear the mask and cothurni, finding the part daily more difficult to bear. I am living on my immense initial momentumвБ†вАФwhile the machinery gradually slows up. My career! Gadzooks.

1913

January 3

From the drawing-room window I see pass almost daily an old gentleman with white hair, a firm step, broad shoulders, healthy pink skin, a sunny smileвБ†вАФalways singing to himself as he goesвБ†вАФa happy, rosy-cheeked old fellow, with a rosy-cheeked mind.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I should like to throw mud at him. By Jove, how I hate him. He makes me wince with my own pain. It is heartless, indecently so, for an old man to be so blithe. Life has, I suppose, never lain in wait for him. The Great Anarchist has spared him a bomb.

January 19

My Aunt, aged seventy-five, who has apparently concluded from my constant absences from Church that my spiritual life is in a parlous way, today read me her portion from a large book with a broad purple-tasseled bookmark. I looked up from вАЬI Promessi SposiвАЭ and said вАЬVery nice.вАЭ It was about someone whose soul was not saved and who would not answer the door when it was knocked. It is jolly to be regarded as a wicked, libidinous youth by an aged maiden Aunt.

January 22

This Diary reads for all the world as if I were not living in mighty London. The truth is I live in a bigger, dirtier cityвБ†вАФill-health. Ill-health, when chronic, is like a permanent ligature around oneвАЩs life. What a fine fellow IвАЩd be if I were perfectly well. My energy for one thing would lift the roof off.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

We conversed around the text: вАЬTo travel hopefully is better than to arrive and true success is to labour.вАЭ She isвБ†вАФwell, so graceful. My God! I love her, I love her, I love her!!!

February 3

A Confession

HвБ†вЄЇ BвБ†вЄЇ invited me to tea to meet his fianc√©e.

Rather pleased with the invitationвБ†вАФI donвАЩt know why, for my idea of myself is greater than my idea of him and probably greater than his idea of himself.

Yet I went and got shaved, and even thought of buying a new pair of gloves, but poverty proved greater than vanity, so I went with naked hands. On arriving at Turnham Green, I removed my spectacles (well knowing how much they damage my personal appearance). However, the beauty of the thing was that, though I waited as agreed, he never turned up, and so I returned home again, crestfallenвБ†вАФand, with my spectacles on again.

February 9

вА¶ вАЬNow, WвБ†вЄЇ, talk to me prettily,вАЭ she said as soon as the door was closed on them.

вАЬOh! make him read a book,вАЭ whined her sister, but we talked of marriage insteadвБ†вАФin all its aspects. Bless their hearts, I found these two dear young things simply sodden with the idea of it.

In the middle I did a knee-jerk which made them scream with laughingвБ†вАФthe patellar reflex was new to them, so I seized a brush from the grate, crossed to Her and gently tapped: out shot her foot, and вЄї cried: вАЬOh, do do it to me as well.вАЭ It was rare fun.

вАЬOh! pretty knee, what do I see?
And he stooped and he tied up my garter for me.вАЭ

February 10

News of ScottвАЩs great adventure! Scott dead a year ago!! The news, when I saw it tonight in the Pall Mall Gazette gave me cold thrills. I could have wept.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What splendid people we humans are! If there be no loving God to watch us, itвАЩs a pity for His sake as much as for our own.

February 15

Tried to kiss her in a taxicab on the way home from the SavoyвБ†вАФthe taxicab danger is very present with usвБ†вАФbut she rejected me quietly, sombrely. I apologised on the steps of the Flats and said I feared I had greatly annoyed her. вАЬIвАЩm not annoyed,вАЭ she said, вАЬonly surprisedвАЭвБ†вАФin a thoughtful, chilly voice.

We had had supper in Soho, and I took some wine, and she looked so bewitching it sent me in a fever, thrumming my fingers on the seat of the cab while she sat beside me impassive. Her shoulders are exquisitely modelled and a beautiful head is carried poised on a tiny neck.

February 16

Walking up the steps to her flat tonight made me pose to HвБ†вЄЇ (who was with me) as Sydney Carton in the picture in A Tale of Two Cities on the steps of the scaffold. He laughed boisterously, as he is delighted to know of my last eveningвАЩs misadventure.

At supper, a story was told of a man who knocked at the door of his ladyвАЩs heart four times and at last was admitted. I remarked that the last part of the romance was weak. She disagreed. HвБ†вЄЇ exclaimed, вАЬOh! but this man has no sentiment at all!вАЭ

вАЬSo much the worse for him,вАЭ chimed in the others.

вАЬHe was 66 years of age,вАЭ added Mrs.¬†вЄї.

вАЬToo old,вАЭ said P. вАЬWhat do you think the best age for a man to marry?вАЭ

H.: вАЬThirty for a man, twenty-five for a woman.вАЭ

She: вАЬThatвАЩs right: it still gives me a little time.вАЭ

P.: вАЬWhat do you think?вАЭ (to me).

I replied sardonicallyвБ†вАФ

вАЬA young man may not yet and an old man not at all.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs right, old wet blanket,вАЭ chirruped PвБ†вЄЇ.

вАЬYou know,вАЭ I continued, delighted to seize the opportunity to assume the role of youthful cynic, вАЬCupid and Death once met at an Inn and exchanged arrows, since when young men have died and old men have doted.вАЭ

HвБ†вЄЇ was charming enough to opine that it was impossible to fix a time for love. Love simply came.

We warned him to be careful on the boat going out.

вАЬYes, I know,вАЭ said HвБ†вЄЇ (who is in love with PвБ†вЄЇ).

вАЬMy brother had a dose of moonlight on board a boat when he sailed and heвАЩs been happy ever since.вАЭ

P.: вАЬHow romantic!вАЭ

H.: вАЬA great passion!вАЭ

вАЬThe only difference,вАЭ I interjected in a sombre monotone, вАЬbetween a passion and a caprice is that the caprice lasts a little longer.вАЭ

вАЬSounds like a book,вАЭ She said in contempt.

It wasвБ†вАФOscar Wilde!

PвБ†вЄЇ insisted on my taking a biscuit. вАЬDonвАЩt mind me,вАЭ she said. вАЬJust think IвАЩm a waitress and take no notice at all.вАЭ

H.: вАЬHumph! I never see him taking no notice of a waitress.вАЭ

(Sneers and Curtain.)

February 24

HвБ†вЄЇ came home last night and told me that she said as he came away, вАЬTell WвБ†вЄЇ I hate him.вАЭ So itвАЩs all right. I shall go over tomorrow againвБ†вАФHurrah! My absence has been felt then.

March 7

Came home, lay on my bed, still dressed, and ruminated.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

First a suspicion then a conviction came to me that I was a cadвБ†вАФa callous, selfish, sensation-hunting cad.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ For the time being the bottom was knocked out of my smug self-satisfaction. For several long half-hours I found myself drifting without compass or stars. I was quite disorientated, temporarily thrown off the balance of my amour propre. Then I got up, lit the gas and looking at myself in the mirror, found it was really trueвБ†вАФI was a mean creature, wholly absorbed in self.

As an act of contrition, I ought to have gone out into the garden and eaten worms. But the mirror brought back my self-consciousness and I began to crawl back into my recently discarded skinвБ†вАФI began to be less loathsome to myself. For as soon as I felt interested or amused or curious over the fact that I had been really loathsome to myself I began to regain my equilibrium. Now, I and myself are on comparatively easy terms with one another. I am settled on the old swivel.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I take a lot of knocking off it and if shot off soon return.

Today, she was silent and melancholy but wonderfully fascinating. One day I am desperate and the next cold and apathetic. Am I in love? God knows! She came to the door to say вАЬGood night,вАЭ and I deliberately strangled my desire to say something.

March 9

In bed till 12:30 reading Bergson and the O.T.

Over to the flat to supper. EвБ†вЄЇ was cold and silent. She spurned me. No wonder. I talked volubly and quite brilliantly with the definite purpose of showing up JвБ†вЄЇвАЩs somnolence. I also pulled his leg. He hates me. No wonder. After supper, he went in to her studio and remained there alone with her while she worked. At 11¬†p.m. he was still there when I came away in a whirlwind of jealousy, regrets, and rage. GвБ†вЄЇ said he was going to stay on until he saw вАЬthe blighter off the premises.вАЭ Neither of us would go in to turn him out.

I love her deeply and once my heart jumped when I thought I heard her coming into the room. But it was only PвБ†вЄЇ. Did not see her againвБ†вАФeven to say вАЬGood night.вАЭ

March 10

Work in the evening in our bedroomвБ†вАФtwo poor miserable bachelorsвБ†вАФHвБ†вЄЇ reading Equity Law, a rug around his legs before an empty grate, while I am sitting at the table in topcoat, with collar up, and writing my magnum opus, which is to bring me fame, fortune andвБ†вАФEвБ†вЄЇ!

HвБ†вЄЇ says that this morning I was putting on my shoes when he pointed out a large hole in the heel of my sock.

вАЬDamn! I shall have to wear boots,вАЭ I saidвБ†вАФat least he says I said it, and I am quite ready to believe him. Such unconsciousness of self is rare with me.

March 15

[At a public dinner at the Holborn Restaurant] JвБ†вЄЇвБ†replied to the toast of the Ladies. Feeble! HвБ†вЄЇ and I stood and had a silent toast to EвБ†вЄЇ and NвБ†вЄЇ by just winking one eye at each other. He sat opposite me.

If I had been asked to reply to this toast I should have said with the greatest gusto, something as followsвБ†вАФ

[Here follows the imaginary speech in full, composed the same night before going to sleep.]

Yet I am taken for a soft fool! My manner is soft, self-conscious, shy. What a lot of self-glorification I lose thereby! What a lot of self-torture I gain in its stead!

March 17

Today went to the B.M. but did very little work. Thought over the matter carefully and decided to ask EвБ†вЄЇ to marry me. Relief to be able to decide. I was happy too.

Yesterday PвБ†вЄЇ came in to us from EвБ†вЄЇвАЩs studio and saidвБ†вАФ

вАЬEвБ†вЄЇ sends her love.вАЭ

вАЬTo whom?вАЭ HвБ†вЄЇ inquired.

вАЬI donвАЩt know,вАЭ PвБ†вЄЇ replied, smiling at me.

March 18

Had a long conversation with HвБ†вЄЇ last night. He says all EвБ†вЄЇ intended to convey was that the quarrel was over.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I felt relieved, because I have no money, butвБ†вАФa large ambition. Then I am selfish, and have not forgotten that I want to spend my holidays in the Jura, and next year three weeks at the Plymouth Laboratory.

March 19

Went over to see EвБ†вЄЇ. We had an awkward half-an-hour alone together. She was looking bewitching! I am plunging more and more into love. Had it on the tip of my tongue once. I am dreadfully fond of her.

вАЬI have a most profound gloom over me,вАЭ I said.

вАЬWhy donвАЩt you try and get rid of it?вАЭ she asked.

вАЬI canвАЩt until Zeus has pity and rolls away the clouds.вАЭ

April 21

We are sitting up in our beds which are side by side in a room on the top story of a boarding house in вЄї Road.

It is 11:30¬†p.m. and I am leaning over one one side lighting the oil lamp so as to boil the kettle to make Ovaltine before going to sleep.

вАЬWhom have I seduced?вАЭ I screamed. вАЬYou rotter, donвАЩt you know that a dead passion full of regrets is as terrible as a dead body full of worms? There, I talk literature, my boy, if you were only Boswell enough to take it down.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ As for KвБ†вЄЇ, I shall never invite him to dinner again. He comes to me and whines that nobody loves him, and so I say, вАШOh! poor lad, never mind, if youвАЩre bored, why, come to my rooms of an evening and hear me talkвБ†вАФyouвАЩll have the time of your life.вАЩ And now heвАЩs cheeky.вАЭ

H. (sipping his drink and very much preoccupied with it) replied abstractedly, вАЬWhen you die youвАЩll go to Hell.вАЭ (I liked his Homeric simplicity.) вАЬYou ought to be buried in a fireproof safe.вАЭ

Silence.

H. (returning to the attack), вАЬI hope she turns you down.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ I said.

вАЬAs for PвБ†вЄЇ,вАЭ he resumed, вАЬsheвАЩs double-Dutch to me.вАЭ

вАЬGo to the Berlitz School,вАЭ I suggested, вАЬand learn the language.вАЭ

вАЬYou bally fool.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ All you do is to sit there and smile like a sanguinary cat. Nothing I say ever rouses you. I believe if I came to you and said, вАШHere, Professor, is a Beetle with 99 legs that has lived on granite in the middle of the Sahara for 40 days and 40 nights,вАЩ youвАЩd simply answer, вАШYes, and that reminds me IвАЩve forgotten to blow my nose.вАЩвАКвАЭ

The two pyjamaed figures shake with laughing, the light goes out and the sanguinary conversation continues on similar lines until we fall asleep.

April 26

Two MonthsвАЩ Sick Leave

In a horrible panicвБ†вАФthe last few daysвБ†вАФI believe I am developing locomotor ataxy. One leg, one arm, and my speech are affected, i.e. the right side and my speech centre. MвБ†вЄЇ is serious.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I hope the disease, whatever it is, will be sufficiently lingering to enable me to complete my book.

RвБ†вЄЇ is a dear man. I shall not easily forget his kindness during this terrible week.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Can the Fates have the audacity?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Who can say?

April 27

I believe there can be no doubt that I have had a slight partial paralysis of my right side (like Dad). I stutter a little in my speech when excited, I cannot write properly (look at this handwriting), and my right leg is rocky at the knee. My head swims.

It is too inconceivably horrible to be buried in the Earth in such splendid spring weather. Who can tell me what is in store for me?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Life opens to me, I catch a glimpse of a vision, and the doors clang to again noiselessly. It is dark. That will be my history. Am developing a passionate belief in my book and a fever of haste to complete it before the cong√© d√©finitif.

April 29

Saw MвБ†вЄЇ again, who said my symptoms were alarming certainly, but he was sure no definite diagnosis could be made.

April 30

Went with MвБ†вЄЇ to see a well-known nerve specialistвБ†вАФDr.¬†HвБ†вЄЇ. He could find no symptoms of a definite disease, though he asked me suspiciously if I had ever been with women.

Ordered two monthsвАЩ complete rest in the country. HвБ†вЄЇ chased me round his consulting room with a drumstick, tapping my nerves and cunningly working my reflexes. Then he tickled the soles of my feet and pricked me with a pinвБ†вАФall of which I stood like a man. He wears a soft black hat, looks like a Quaker, and reads the Verhandlungen d. Gesellschaft d. Nervenarzten.

MвБ†вЄЇ is religious and after I had disclosed my physique to him yesterday (for the 99th time) he remained on his knees by the couch in his consulting room (after working my reflexes) for a moment or two in the attitude of prayer. When the Doctor prays for youвБ†вАФbetter call in the undertaker. My epitaph вАЬHe played Ludo well.вАЭ The game anyhow requires moral staminaвБ†вАФask HвБ†вЄЇ.

May 5

At RвБ†вЄЇ. Mugged about all day. Put on a gramophone recordвБ†вАФthen crawled up into a corner of the large, empty drawing room and ate my heart out. Heart has a bitter tasteвБ†вАФif itвАЩs your own.

May 6

Sat in the вАЬmorning roomвАЭ feeling ill. In the chair opposite sat Aunt Fanny, aged 86, knitting. I listened to the click of her needles, while out in the garden a thrush sang, and there was a red sunset.

May 8

Before I left RвБ†вЄЇ, AвБ†вЄЇ [My brother] had written to Uncle enclosing my doctorвАЩs letter. I donвАЩt know the details except that Dr.¬†MвБ†вЄЇ emphasised the seriousness and yet held out hope that two monthsвАЩ rest would allay the symptoms.

May 11

At Home

I made some offensive remark to HвБ†вЄЇ whom I met in the street. This set him off.

вАЬYou blighter, I hope you marry a loose woman. May your children be all bandy-legged and squint-eyed, may your teeth drop out, and your toes have bunions,вАЭ and so on in his usual lengthy commination.

I turned to the third man.

вАЬBobвБ†вАФthis!вБ†вАФafter all IвАЩve done for that young man! I have even gone out of my way to cultivate in him a taste for poetryвБ†вАФuntil he is now, in fact, quite wrapped up in itвБ†вАФindeed, so much so, that for a time he was nothing but a brown paper parcel labelled Poetry.вАЭ

H. (doggedly): вАЬWhen are you going to die?вАЭ

вАЬThat Master HвБ†вЄЇ,вАЭ I answered menacingly, вАЬis on the knees of the Gods.вАЭ

H.: вАЬI shanвАЩt believe youвАЩre dead till I see your tombstone. I shall then say to the Sexton, вАШIs he really dead, then?вАЩ and the Sexton will say, вАШWell, вАЩeeвАЩs buried onny way.вАЩвАКвАЭ

Bob was not quite in sympathy with our boisterous spirits.

May 15

Gardening

Sought out HвБ†вЄЇ as he was watering his petunias in the garden. He informed me he was going to London on Monday.

H.: вАЬMother is coming too.вАЭ

B.: вАЬWhy?вАЭ

H.: вАЬOh! IвАЩm buying my kitвБ†вАФshirts and things. I sail at the beginning of July.вАЭ

B.: вАЬI suppose shirts are difficult to buy. You wouldnвАЩt know what to do with one if you had one. Your mother will lead you by the hand into a shop and say, вАШHвБ†вЄЇ, dear, this is a shirt,вАЩ and youвАЩll reply with pathos, вАШMother what are the wild shirts saying?вАЩвАКвАЭ

H.: вАЬYouвАЩre a B.F.вАЭ (goes on watering).

вАЬI wonder what youвАЩd do if you were let loose in a big garden,вАЭ I began.

H.: вАЬI should be as happy as a bird. I should hop about, chirrup and lay eggs. You should have seen my tomato plants last yearвБ†вАФone was as tall as father.вАЭ

B.: вАЬNow tell me of the Gooseberry as big as Mother.вАЭ

Mutual execrations. Then we grinned and cackled at each other, emitting weird and ferocious cachinnations. Several times a day in confidential, serious tonesвБ†вАФafter one of these explosionsвБ†вАФwe say, вАЬI really believe weвАЩre mad.вАЭ You never heard such extraordinary caterwaulings. Our snappy conversations are interrupted with them every minute or so!

Stagnancy

A stagnant day. Lay still in the Park all day with just sufficient energy to observe. The Park was almost empty. Everyone but me at work. Nothing is more dreary than a pleasure ground on workdays. There was one man a little way off throwing a ball to a clever dog. Behind me on the path, someone came along wheeling a pram. I listened in a kind of coma to the scrunching of the gravel in the distance a long time after the pram was out of sight. Far awayвБ†вАФthe tinkle of Church bells in a village across the river, and, in front, the man still throwing the ball to his clever dog.

May 25

Death

вА¶ I suppose the truth is I am at last broken to the idea of Death. Once it terrified me and once I hated it. But now it only annoys me. Having lived with the Bogey for so long, and broken bread with him so often, I am used to his ugliness, though his persistent attentions bore me. Why doesnвАЩt he do it and have done with me? Why this deference, why does he pass me everything but the poison? Why am I such an unconscionably long time dying?

What embitters me is the humiliation of having to die, to have to be pouring out the precious juices of my life into the dull Earth, to be no longer conscious of what goes on, no longer moving abroad upon the Earth creating attraction and repulsions, pouring out oneвАЩs ego in a stream. To think that the women I have loved will be marrying and forget, and that the men I have hated will continue on their way and forget I ever hated themвБ†вАФthe ignominy of being dead! What voluble talker likes his mouth to be stopped with earth, who relishes the idea of the carrion worm mining in the seat of the intellect?

May 29

Renunciation

Staying at the KingвАЩs Hotel, вЄї. Giddiness very bad. Death seems unavoidable. A tumour on the brain?

Coming down here in the train, sat in corner of the compartment, twined one leg around the other, rested my elbow on the window ledge, and gazed out helplessly at the exuberant green fields, green woods, and green hedgerows. The weather was perfect, the sun blazed down.

Certainly, I was rather sorry for myself at the thought of leaving it all. But I girded up my loins and wrapped around me for a while the mantle of a nobler sentiment; i.e. I felt sorry for the others as wellвБ†вАФfor the two brown carters in the road ambling along with a timber wagon, for the two old maids in the same compartment with me knitting bedsocks, for the beautiful Swallows darting over the stream, for the rabbit that lopped into the fern just as we passedвБ†вАФthey too were all leaving it.

The extent of my benign compassion startled meвБ†вАФit was so unexpected. Perhaps for the first time in my life I forgot all about my own miserable ambitionsвБ†вАФI forgave the successful, the timeservers, the self-satisfied, the overweening, the gracious and condescendingвБ†вАФall, in fact, who hitherto have been thorns in my flesh and innocently enough have goaded me to still fiercer efforts to win through. вАЬPoor people,вАЭ I said. вАЬLeave them alone. Let them be happy if they can.вАЭ With a submissive heart, I was ready to sit down in the rows of this worldвАЩs failures and never have thought one bitter word about success. To all those persons who in one way or another had foiled my purposes I extended a pardon with Olympian gravity, and, strangest of all, I could have melted such frosty moral rectitudes with a genuine interest in the careers of my struggling contemporaries. With perfect self-abnegation, I held out my hand to them and wished them all вАЬGod Speed.вАЭ

It was a strange metempsychosis. Yet of a truth it is no use being niggardly over our lives. We are all of us вАЬshelling out.вАЭ And we can afford to be generous, for we shall allвБ†вАФsome early, some lateвБ†вАФbe bankrupt in the end. For my part, IвАЩve had a short and boisterous voyage and shanвАЩt be sorry to get into port. I give up all my plans, all my hopes, all my loves and enthusiasms without remonstrance. I renounce allвБ†вАФI myself am already really dead.

May 30

Last night the sea was as flat as a pavement, a pretty barque with all her sails out to catch the smallest puff of windвБ†вАФthe tiniest inspirationвБ†вАФwas nevertheless without motionвБ†вАФa painted ship on a tapestry of violet. HвБ†вЄЇ Hill was an immense angular mass of indigo blue. Even rowing boats made little progress and the water came off the languid paddles in syrupy clots. Everything was utterly still, the air thickвБ†вАФlike cotton wool to the touch and very stifling; vitality in living things leaked away under a sensuous lotus influence. Intermittently after the darkness had come, Bullpoint Lighthouse shone like the wink of a lascivious eye.

Pottering about all day on the Pier and Front, listening to other peopleвАЩs talk, catching snippets of conversationвБ†вАФnot edifying. If there were seven wise men in the town, I would not save it. Damn the place!

May 31

вА¶ I espied her first in the distance and turned my head away quickly and looked out to sea. A moment after, I began to turn my head round again slowly with the cautiousness and air of suspicion of a Tortoise poking its head out from underneath his shell. I was terrified to discover that in the meantime she had come and sat down on the seat immediately behind me with her back to mine. We sat like this back to back for some time and I enjoyed the novel experience and the tension. A few years ago, the bare sight of her gave me palpitation of the heart, and, on the first occasion that I had the courage to stop to speak, I felt livid and the skin on my face twitched uncontrollably.

Presently I got up and walked pastвБ†вАФin the knowledge that she must now be conscious of my presence after a disappearance of three years. Later we met face to face and I broke the ice. SheвАЩs a pretty girl.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ So too is her sister.

Few people, except my barber, know how amorous I am. He has to shave my sinuous lips.

June 3

Spent many dreadful hours cogitating whether to accept their invitation to dinner.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I wanted to go for several reasons. I wanted to see her in a home-setting for the first time, and I wanted to spend the evening with three pretty girls. I also had the idea of displaying myself to the scrutinising gaze of the family as the hero of the old romance: and of showing Her how much I had progressed since last we met and what a treasure she had lost.

On the other hand, I was afraid that the invitation was only a casual one, I feared a snuffy reception, a frosty smile and a rigid hand. Could I go up and partake of meat at their board, among brothers and sisters taking me for an ogre of a jilt, and she herself perhaps opposite me making me blush perpetually to recall our onetime passionate kisses, our love letters and our execrable verses to each other! There seemed dreadful possibilities in such an adventure. Yet I badly wanted to experience the piquant situation.

At 7¬†p.m., half an hour before I was due, decided on strong measures. I entered a pub and took a stiff whisky and soda, and then set off with a stout heart to take the icy family by stormвБ†вАФand if need be live down my evil reputation by my amiability and urbanity!

I wentвБ†вАФand of course everything passed off in the most normal manner. She is a very pretty girlвБ†вАФlike velvet. Before dinner, we walked in the gardenвБ†вАФand talked only of flowers.

June 4

On the Hill, this morning, felt the thrill of the news of my own Death: I mean I imagined I heard the wordsвБ†вАФ

вАЬYouвАЩve heard the news about BвБ†вЄЇ?вАЭ

Second Voice: вАЬNo, what?вАЭ

вАЬHeвАЩs dead.вАЭ

Silence.

WonвАЩt all this seem piffle if I donвАЩt die after all! As an artist in life I ought to die; it is the only artistic endingвБ†вАФand I ought to die now or the Third Act will fizzle out in a long doctorвАЩs bill.

June 5

A New Pile in the Pier

Watched some men put a new pile in the pier. There was all the usual paraphernalia of chains, pulleys, cranes, and ropes, with a massive wooden pile swinging over the water at the end of a long wire hawser. Everything was in the massive styleвБ†вАФeven the menвБ†вАФvery powerful men, slow, ruminative, silent men.

Nothing very relevant could be gathered from casual remarks. The conversation was without exception monosyllabic: вАЬLet go,вАЭ or вАЬStand fast.вАЭ But by close attention to certain obscure movements of the man on the ladder near the waterвАЩs edge, it gradually came through to my consciousness that all these powerful, silent men were up against some bitter difficulty. I cannot say what it was. The burly monsters were silent about the matter.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ In fact they appeared almost indifferentвБ†вАФand tired, oh! so very tired of the whole business. The attitude of the man nearest me was that for all he cared the pile could go on swinging in midair to the crack of Doom.

They continued slow, laborious efforts to overcome the secret difficulty. But these gradually slackened and finally ceased. One massive man after another abandoned his post in order to lean over the rails and gaze like a mystic into the depths of the sea. No one spoke. No one saw anything not even in the depths of the sea. One spat, and with round, sad eyes contemplated the trajectory of his brown bolus (he had been chewing) in its descent into the water.

The foreman, an original thinker, lit a cigarette, which relieved the tension. Then, slowly and with majesty, he turned on his heel, and walked away. With the sudden eclipse of the foremanвАЩs interest, the incident closed. I should have been scarcely surprised to find him behind the Harbour-masterвАЩs Office playing вАЬShove-haвАЩpennyвАЭ or skittles with the pile still swinging in midair.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ After all it was only a bloody pile.

June 11

Depression

Suffering from depression.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The melancholy fit fell very suddenly. All the colour went out of my life, the world was dirty gray. On the way back to my hotel caught sight of HвБ†вЄЇ, jumping into a cab, after a visit to SвБ†вЄЇ Sands. But the sight of him aroused no desire in me to shout or wave. I merely wondered how on earth he could have spent a happy day at such a Sandy place.

On arriving at вЄї, sank deeper into my morass. It suffocated me to find the old familiar landmarks coming into viewвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the holidaymakers along the streets how I hated themвБ†вАФthe Peg Top Hill how desolateвБ†вАФall as beforeвБ†вАФhow dull. The very fact that they were all there as before in the morning nauseated me. The seacoast here is magnificent, the town is prettyвБ†вАФI know that, of course. But all looked dreary and cheerlessвБ†вАФjust the sort of feeling one gets on entering an empty house with no fire on a winterвАЩs day and nowhere to sit down.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I felt as lonely and desolate as a man suddenly fallen from the clouds into an unknown town on the Antarctic Continent built of ice and inhabited by Penguins. Who are these people? I asked myself irritably. There perhaps on the other side of the street was my own brother. But I was not even faintly interested and told the cabman to drive on. The spray from the sea fogged my spectacles and made me weary.

June 14

The Restlessness of the Sea

The restlessness of the sea acts as a soporific on jangled nerves. You gaze at its incessant activities, unwillingly at first because they distract your attention from your own cherished worries and griefsвБ†вАФbut later you watch with complete self-abandonвБ†вАФit wrenches you out of yourselfвБ†вАФand eventually with a kind of stupid hypnotic stare.

Dr.¬†Spurgeon

The day has been overcast, but tonight a soft breeze sprang up and swept the sky clear as softly as a mop. The sun coming out shone upon a white sail far out in the channel, scarcely another vessel hove in sight. The white sail glittered like a piece of silver paper whenever the mainsail swung round as the vessel tacked. Its solitariness and whiteness in a desert of marine blue attracted the attention and held it till at last I could look at nothing else. The sight of itвБ†вАФso clean and white and fairвБ†вАФset me yearning for all the rarest and most exquisite things my imagination could conjure upвБ†вАФa beautiful girl, with fair and sunburnt skin, brown eyes, dark eyebrows, and small pretty feet; a dewdrop in a violetвАЩs face; an orange-tip butterfly swinging on an umbel of a flower.

The sail went on twinkling and began to exert an almost moral influence over me. It drew out all the good in me. I longed to follow it on white wingsвБ†вАФan angel I supposeвБ†вАФto quit this husk of a body вАЬas raiment put away,вАЭ and pursue Truth and Beauty across the sea to the horizon, and beyond the horizon up the sky itself to its last tenuous confines, no doubt with a still small voice summoning me and the rest of the elect to an Agapemone, with Dr.¬†Spurgeon at the door distributing tracts.

I can scoff like this now. But at the time my exaltation was very real. My soul strained in the leash. I was full of a desire for unattainable spiritual beauty. I wanted something. But I donвАЩt know what I want.

June 16

My Sense of Touch

My sense of touch has always been morbidly acute. I like to feel a cigarette locked in the extreme corner of my mouth. When I remove it from my mouth then I hold it probably up in the fork between two fingers. If I am waiting for a meal I finger the cool knives and forks. If I am in the country I plunge my hands with outspread fingers into a mass of large-topped grasses, then close my fingers, crush and decapitate the lot.

June 27

Camping Out at SвБ†вЄЇ Sands

A brilliant summer day. Up early, breakfasted, and, clad in sweater and trousers, walked up the sands to the boathouse with bare feet.

Everything was wonderful! I strode along over the level sands infatuated with the sheer ability to put one leg in front of the other and walk. I loved to feel the muscles of my thighs working, and to swing my arms in rhythm with the stride. The stiff breeze had blown the sky clear, and was rushing through my long hair, and bellowing into each ear. I strode as Alexander must have done!

Then I stretched my whole length out along a flat plank on the sands, which was as dry as a bone and warm. There was not a soul on the sands. Everything was bare, clean, windswept. My plank had been washed clean and white. The sandsвБ†вАФ3 miles of itвБ†вАФwere hard and purified, level. My eye raced along in every directionвБ†вАФthere was nothingвБ†вАФnot a bird or a manвБ†вАФto stop it. In that immense windswept space nothing was present save me and the wind and the seaвБ†вАФa flattering moment for the egotist.

At the foot of the cliffs on the return journey met an old man gathering sticks. As he ambled along dropping sticks into a long sack he called out casually, вАЬDo you believe in Jesus Christ?вАЭ in the tone of voice in which one would say, вАЬI think we shall have some rain before night.вАЭ вАЬAye, aye,вАЭ came the answer without hesitation from a boy lying on his back in the sands a few yards distant, вАЬand that He died to save me.вАЭ

Life is full of surprises like this. The only other sounds I have heard today were the Herring GullвАЩs cackle. Your own gardener will one day look over his rake and give you the correct chemical formula for carbonic acid gas. I met a postman once reading Shelley as he walked his rounds.

June 28

I am writing this by the lamp in the cabin among the sand hills waiting for HвБ†вЄЇ to arrive from town with provisions. I wear a pair of bags, a dirty sweater, and go without hat or shoes and stockings. There is a вАЬDeadwood DickвАЭ atmosphere here. IвАЩm a sort of bronco-breaker or rancher off duty writing home. In a minute I havenвАЩt the slightest doubt, HвБ†вЄЇ will gallop into the compound, tether his colt and come in вАЬraising CainвАЭ for a belly-full of red meat.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If I am going to live after all (touch wood) I shall go abroad and be in the open.

I eat greedily, am getting very sunburnt, am growing hairy (that means strength!), and utter portentous oaths. If I stayed here much longer I should grow a tail and climb trees.

After a supper of fried eggs and fried bread done to a nicety, turned in at ten, and both of us lay warm and comfortable in bed, smoking cigarettes and listening to HoffmannвАЩs Barcarolle on the gramophone. We put the lamp out, and it pleased us to watch the glow of each otherвАЩs cigarettes in the dark.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Neither of us spoke.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Went to sleep at midnight. Awoke at sunrise to hear an Owl still hooting, a Lark singing, and several Jackdaws clattering on our tin roof with their claws as they walked.

July 1

In London Again

Returned to London very depressed. Am not so well as I was three weeks ago. The sight of one eye is affected, and I am haunted by the possibility of blindness. Then I have a numb feeling on one side of my face, and my right arm is less mobile.

Left darling Mother in a very weak state in bed, with neuritis and a weak heart. She cried when I said вАЬGoodbye,вАЭ and asked me to go to Church as often as I could, and to read a portion of Scripture every day. I promised. Then she added, вАЬFor DadвАЩs sake;вАЭ just as if I would not do it for her. Poor dear, she suffers a deal of pain. She does not know how ill I am. I have not told her.

July 3

Back at work. A terrible day. Thoughts of suicideвБ†вАФa pistol.

July 8

I get through each day with the utmost difficulty. I have to wrestle with every minute. Each hour is a conquest. The three quarters of an hour at lunch comes as a Godsend. I look forward to it all the morning, I enter into it with joyful relief with no thought of the dreadful moment impending when I must return and reenter my room. By being wise like this, I manage to husband my spirits and am relatively cheerful for one hour in the middle of each difficult day.

July 9

Several times I have gone to bed and hoped I should never wake up. Life grows daily more impossible. Today I put a slide underneath the microscope and looked at it. It was like looking at something through the wrong end of a telescope. I sat with eye glued to the ocular, so as to keep up a pretence of work in case someone came in. My mind was occupied with quite different affairs. If one is pondering on Life and Death, it is a terrible task to have to study Mites.

July 10

Am doing no work at all.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I sit motionless in my chair and beat the devilвАЩs tattoo with my thumbs and think, think, think in the same horrible circle hour after hour. I am unable to work. I havenвАЩt the courage to. IвАЩve lost my nerve.

At five I return вАЬhomeвАЭ to the Boardinghouse and get more desperate.

Two old maids sat down to dinner tonight, one German youth (a lascivious, ranting, brainless creature), a lady typist (who takes drugs they say), a dipsomaniac (who has monthly boutsвБ†вАФHвБ†вЄЇ carried him upstairs and put him to bed the other night), two invertebrate violinists who play in the Covent Garden Orchestra, a colonial lady engaged in a bedroom intrigue with a man who sits at my table. What are these people to me? I hate them all. They know it and are offended.

After dinner, put on my cap and rushed out anywhere to escape. Walked to the end of the street, not knowing where I was going or what doing. Stopped and stared with fixed eyes at the traffic in Kensington Road, undetermined what to do with myself and unable to make up my mind (volitional paralysis). Turned round, walked home, and went straight to bed 9¬†p.m., anxiously looking forward to tomorrow evening when I go to see her again, but at the same time wondering how on earth I am to get through tomorrowвАЩs round before the evening comes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ This is a hand-to-mouth existence. My own inner life is scorching up all outside interests. Zoology appears as a curious thing in a Baghdad bazaar. I sit in my room at the B.M. and play with it; I let it trickle through my fingers and roll away like a child playing with quicksilver.

July 11

Over to the flat. She was looking beautiful in a black dress, with a white silk blouse, and a Byron collar, negligently open in front as if a button had come out. She said I varied: sometimes I went up in her estimation, sometimes down; once I went down very low. I understood her to say I was now up! Alleluia!

July 14

вА¶ It would take too long and I am too tired to write out all the varying phases of this dayвАЩs lifeвБ†вАФall its impressions and petty miseries chasing one another across my consciousness or leapfrogging over my chest like gleeful fiends.8

July 21

Thoroughly enjoyed the journey up to town this morning. I secretly gloated over the fact that the train was dashing along over the rails to London bearing me and all the rest of the trainвАЩs company upon their pursuitsвБ†вАФwealth, fame, learning. I was inebriated with the speed, ferocity, and dash of living.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If the train had charged into the buffers I should have hung my head out of the window and cheered. If a man had got in my way, IвАЩd have knocked him down. The wheels of the carriage were singing a lusty song in which I joined.

July 30

вА¶ We talked of men and women, and she said she thought men were neither angels nor devils but just men. I said I thought women were either angels or devils.

вАЬI am afraid to ask you which you think me.вАЭ

вАЬYou neednвАЩt,вАЭ I said shortly.

August 9

Horribly upset with news from home. Mother is really ill. The Doctor fears serious nerve trouble and says she will always be an invalid. This is awful, poor dear! ItвАЩs dreadful, and yet I have a tiny wish buried at the bottom of my heart that she may be removed early from us rather than linger in pain of body and mind. Especially do I hope she may not live to hear any grievous news of me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What irony that she should lose the use of her right arm only two years after DadвАЩs death from paralysis. It is cruel for it reminds her of DadвАЩs illness.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What, too, would she think if she could have heard MвБ†вЄЇвАЩs first words to me yesterday on one of my periodical visits to his consulting room, вАЬWell, howвАЩs the paralysis?вАЭ

In the evening went over to see her. She was wearing a black silk gown and looked handsome.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She is always the same sombre, fascinating, lissom, soft-voiced She! She herself never changes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What am I to do? I cannot give her up and yet I do not altogether wish to take her to my heart. It distresses me to know how to proceed. I am a wily fish.

August 10

Sat in the gardens with her. We sat facing the sun for a while until she was afraid of developing freckles and turned around, deliberately turning her back on good King Sol.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I said it was disrespectful.

вАЬOh! he doesnвАЩt mind,вАЭ she said. вАЬHeвАЩs a dear. He kissed me and said, вАШTurn round my dear if you like.вАЩвАКвАЭ

IsnвАЩt she tantalising?

I wanted to say sarcastically, вАЬI wonder you let him kiss you,вАЭ but there was a danger of the remark reviving the dead.

August 14

I tried my best, IвАЩve sought every loophole of escape, but I am quite unable to avoid the melancholy fact that her thumbs areвБ†вАФlamentable. I am genuinely upset about it for I like her. No one more than I would be more delighted if they were otherwise.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Poor dear! how I love her! ThatвАЩs why IвАЩm so concerned about her thumbs.

August 21

A wire from AвБ†вЄЇ came at 11:50 saying вАЬDarling Mother passed peacefully away yesterday afternoon.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yesterday afternoon I was writing Zoology and all last night I slept soundly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was quite sudden. Caught the first train home.

August 23

The funeral.

August 31

Staying at the Hotel du Guesclin at Cancale near St.¬†Malo with my dear AвБ†вЄЇ.

This flood of new experiences has knocked my diary habit out of gear. To be candid, IвАЩve forgotten all about myself. IвАЩve been too engrossed in living to stand the strain of setting down and in cold blood writing out all the things seen and heard. If I once began I should blow through these pages like a whirlwind.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But what a waste of time with M. le batelier waiting outside with his bisque to take us mackerel fishing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

September 8

Returned to Southampton yesterday. Have spent the night at Okehampton in Devonshire en route for TвБ†вЄЇ Rectory. This morning we hatched the ridiculous idea of hiring two little Dartmoor ponies and riding out from the town. AвБ†вЄЇ rides fairly well though he has not been astride a beast for years. As for me, I cannot ride at all! Yet I had the idea that I could easily manage a pretty little pony with brown eyes and a long tail. On going out into the Inn yard, was horrifiedвБ†вАФtwo horses saddledвБ†вАФone a large traction beast.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I climbed on to the smaller one, walked him out of the yard and down the road in good style without accident. Once in the country, however, my animal, the fresher of the two, insisted on a smart trot which shook me up a good deal so that I hardly kept my seat. This eventually so annoyed the animal that it began to fidget and zigzag across the roadвБ†вАФno doubt preparing to break away at a stretch gallop when once it had rid itself of the incomprehensible pair of legs across its back.

I got off quickly and swapped horses with AвБ†вЄЇ.

Walked him most of the way, while AвБ†вЄЇ cantered forward and back to cheer me on. Ultimately however this beast, too, got sick of walking and began to trot. For a time I stood this well and began to rise in my saddle quite nicely. After two miles, horrible soreness supervened, and I had to get offвБ†вАФvery carefully, with a funny feeling in my legsвБ†вАФeven looked down at them to assure myself they were not bandy! In doing so, the horseвБ†вАФthis traction monsterвБ†вАФstepped on my toe and I swore.

On nearing the village, LвБ†вЄЇ arrived, riding AвБ†вЄЇвАЩs animal and holding his sides for laughing at me as I crawled along holding the carthorse by the bridle. Got on again and rode into the Rectory grounds in fine style like a dashing cavalier, everyone jeering at me from the lawn.

September 28

Having lived on this planet now for the space of twenty-four years, I can claim with some cogency that I am qualified to express some sort of opinion about it. I therefore hereby record that I find myself in an absorbingly interesting place where I live, move and have my being, dominated by one monstrous feature above all othersвБ†вАФthe mystery of it all! Everything is so astonishing, my own existence so incredible!

Nothing explains itself. Everyone is dumb. It is like walking about at a masqued Ball.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Even I myself am a mystery to me. How wonderful and frightening that isвБ†вАФto feel yourselfвБ†вАФyour innermost and most substantial possession to be a mystery, incomprehensible. I look at myself in the mirror and mock at myself. On some days I am to myself as strange and unfamiliar as a Pterodactyl. There is a certain grim humour in finding myself here possessed of a perfectly arbitrary arrangement of lineaments when I never asked to be here and never selected my own attributes. To the dignity of a human being it seems like a coarse practical joke.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ My own freakish physique is certainly a joke.

October 4

In London Again

KвБ†вЄЇ comes in from her dancing class, nods to me, hugs her sister around the neck and saysвБ†вАФ

вАЬOh! you dear thing, youвАЩve got a cold.вАЭ

вАЬI shouldnвАЩt do that,вАЭ I remark, green-eyed, вАЬsheвАЩs in an awful wax tonight.вАЭ

She: вАЬOh! I donвАЩt mind KвБ†вЄЇ!вАЭ

(Laughter!)

October 8

Heard a knock at the door last night, and, thinking it was RвБ†вЄЇ, I unbolted it and let in a tramp who at once asked God to bless me and crown all my sorrow with joy. An amiable fellow to be sureвБ†вАФso I gave him some coppers and he at once repeated with wonderful fervour, вАЬGod bless you, sir.вАЭ

вАЬI wish He would,вАЭ I answered, вАЬI have a horrible cold.вАЭ

вАЬAh, I know, I gets it myself and the hinfluenzaвБ†вАФhave you had that, sir?вАЭ

In ten minutes I should have told him all my personal history. But he was thirsting for a drink and went off quickly and left me with my heart unburdened. London is a lonely place.

Today journeyed to вЄї where I gave evidence as an expert in Economic Entomology at the County Court in a case concerning damage to furniture by mites for which I am paid ¬£8 8s. fee and expenses and travelled first class. What irony!9

October 11

I may be a weak, maundering, vacillating fool but I cannot help loving her on one day, being indifferent the next and on some occasions even disliking her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Today she was charming, with a certain warm glossy perfection on her face and hair.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And she loves meвБ†вАФI could swear it. вАЬAnd when a woman woosвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ etc. How difficult for a vain and lonely man to resist her. She tells me many times in many dainty ways that she loves me without so much as stopping her work to talk.

I wish I were permanently and irresistibly enamoured. I want a bouleversement.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

October 13

Went to see a Harley Street oculist about the sight of one eye, which has caused a lot of trouble and worry of late and continuously haunted me with the possibility of blindness. At times, I see men as trees walking and print becomes hopelessly blurred.

The Specialist however is reassuring. The eye is healthyвБ†вАФno neuritisвБ†вАФbut the adjustment muscles have been thrown out of gear by the nervous troubles of last spring.

Was ever man more sorely tempted? Here am I lonely and uncomfortable in diggings with a heart like nascent oxygen.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Shall I? Yes, but.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And I have neither health nor wealth.

October 22

The British Museum Reading Room

I saw it for the first time today! Gadzooks!! This is the only fit ejaculation to express my amazement! ItвАЩs a pagan temple with the Gods in the middle and all around, various obscure dark figures prostrating themselves in worship.

For anyone who is not simply a Sheep or Cow or whose nervous organisation is a degree more sensitive than the village blacksmithвАЩs, it is a besetting peril to his peace of mind to be constantly moving about an independent being, with loves and hates, and a separate identity among other separate identities, who prowl and prowl around like the hosts of MidianвБ†вАФready to snarl, fight, seize you, bore you, exasperate you, to arouse all your passions, call up all the worst from the depths where they have lain hidden.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A day spent among my fellows goads me to a frenzy by the evening. I am no longer fit for human companionship. People string me up to concert pitch. I develop suspicions of one that he is prying, of another that he patronises. Others make me horribly anxious to stand well in their eyes and horribly curious to know what they think of me. Others I hate and loatheвБ†вАФfor no particular reason. There is a man I am acquainted with concerning whom I know nothing at all. He may be Jew, Gentile, Socinian, Pre-adamite, Anabaptist, RosicrucianвБ†вАФI donвАЩt know, and I donвАЩt care, for I hate him. I should like to smash his face in. I donвАЩt know why.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ In the whole course of our tenuous acquaintance we have spoken scarce a dozen words to each other. Yet I should like to blow up his face with dynamite. If I had ¬£200 a year private income I should be in wait for him tomorrow round a corner and land him oneвБ†вАФjust to indicate my economic independence. He would call for the police and the policemanвБ†вАФdiscerning creatureвБ†вАФon arrival, would surely say, вАЬWith a face like that, IвАЩm not surprised.вАЭ

RвБ†вЄЇ said to me this morning, вАЬWell, have you heard?вАЭ with an exuberance of curiosity that made my blood boilвБ†вАФhe was referring to my Essay still at the bar of the opinion of the Editor of the English Review. вАЬYou beast,вАЭ I snapped and walked off.

RвБ†вЄЇ shouted with laughter for he realizes my anger with him is only semi-serious: it is meant and not meant: meant, for it is justified by the facts; not meant, for I canвАЩt be too serious over anything au fond.

Of all the grim and ridiculous odds and ends of chance that Fortune has rolled up to my feet, my friendship with a man like BвБ†вЄЇ is the grimmest and most ridiculous. He is a bachelor of sixty, rather good-looking, of powerful physique and a faultless constitution.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ His ignorance is colossal and he once asked whether Australia, for example, though surrounded by water, is not connected up with other land underneath the sea. Being himself a child in intelligence (though commercially cunning), he has a great respect for my brains. Being himself a strong man, he views my ill-health with much contempt. His private opinion is that I am in consumption. When asked once by a lady if I were not going to be вАЬa great manвАЭ one day, he replied, вАЬYesвБ†вАФif he lives.вАЭ I ought to walk six miles a day, drink a bottle of stout with my dinner, and eat plenty of onions. His belief in the curative properties of onions is strong as death.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

His system of prophylaxis may be quickly summarisedвБ†вАФ

  1. Hot whisky ad lib and off to bed.

  2. A woman.

These two sterling preventives he has often urged upon me at the same time tipping out a quantity of anathemas on doctors and physic.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He is a cynic. He scoffs at the medical profession, the Law, the Church, the Press. Every man is guilty until he is proved innocent. The Premier is an unscrupulous character, the Bishop a salacious humbug. No doctor will cure, for it pays him to keep you ill. Every clergyman puts the Sunday-school teacher in the family way. His mouth is permanently distorted by cynicism.

He is vain and believes all women are in love with him. When playing the Gallant, he turns on a special voice, wears white spats, and looks like a Newmarket вАЬCrook.вАЭ

вАЬI lost my bus,вАЭ a girl says to him. вАЬLost your bust,вАЭ he answers, in broad Scotch. вАЬI canвАЩt see that youвАЩve done that.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ His sexual career has been a remarkable one, he claiming to have brought many women to bed, and actually to have lain with women of almost all European nationalities, for he has been a great traveller.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

This man is my devoted friend!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And truth to tell I get on with him better than I do with most people. I like his gamey flavour, his utter absence of self-consciousness, and his doggy loyalty to myselfвБ†вАФhis weaker brother. He may be depraved in his habits, coarse in his language, boorish in his manners, ludicrous in the wrongness of all his views. But I like him just because he is so hopeless. I get on with him because it is so impossible to reclaim himвБ†вАФmy missionary spirit is not intrigued. If he only dabbled in vice (for an experiment), if he had pale, watery ideas about current literatureвБ†вАФifвБ†вАФto use his own favourite epithetвБ†вАФhe were genteel, I should quarrel.

October 30

Having developed a passion for a piece of sculpture by R. Boeltzig called the ReifenwerferinвБ†вАФthe most beautiful figure of a woman. I am already devoted to RodinвАЩs Kiss and have a photo of it framed in my bedroom. Have written to BrucianiвАЩs.

I suspect that my growing appreciation of the plastic art is with me only distilled sensuality. I enjoy my morning bath for the same reason. My bath is a daily baptism. I revel in the pleasure of the pain of the cold water. I whistle gleefully because I am clean and cool and nude early in the morning with the sun still low, before the day has been stained by clothes, dirt, pain, exasperation, death.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How I love myself as I rub myself down!вБ†вАФthe cool, pink skinвБ†вАФI could eat it! I want to be all day in a cold bath to enjoy the pain of mortifying the fleshвБ†вАФit is so beautiful, so soft, so inscrutableвБ†вАФif I cut out chunks of it, it would only bleed.

November 8

The other morning RвБ†вЄЇ said hyperbolically that he hadnвАЩt slept all night for fear that, before he had time to put an arresting hand on my shoulder and say вАЬDonвАЩt,вАЭ I might have gone and become вАЬEntangled.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вА¶ No, IвАЩm as firm as a rock, my dear. But in imagination the affair was continued as followsвБ†вАФ

She: вАЬI am fond of you, you know.вАЭ

He: вАЬI wish you wouldnвАЩt say these things to meвБ†вАФtheyвАЩre quite embarrassing.вАЭ

She: вАЬOh! my dear, IвАЩm not serious, you knowвБ†вАФyouвАЩre such a vain young man.вАЭ

He: вАЬWell, itвАЩs equally embarrassing anyway.вАЭ

She: вАЬThen I am serious.вАЭ

Tears.

I say: вАЬI wish you would take me only for what I amвБ†вАФa blackguard with no good intentions, yet no very evil onesвБ†вАФbut still a blackguard, whom you seem to find has engaging manners.вАЭ

I breathe freely hoping to have escaped this terrible temptation and turn to go. But she, looking up smiling through a curtain of wet eyelashes, asksвБ†вАФ

вАЬWonвАЩt the blackguard stop a little longer?вАЭ In a moment my earth works, redoubts, and bastions fall down, I rush forward impetuously into her arms shouting, вАЬI will, I will, I will as long as for eternity.вАЭ

(Curtain.)
